us-politics.headline: Trump Threatens Iran With War Over Strait of Hormuz
us-politics.body: President Trump posted an Easter message threatening Iran with 'Hell' if it moves to close the Strait of Hormuz, and declined to rule out ground troops if no deal is reached. The threat came as Iran confirmed a US aircraft was destroyed during a recovery mission for a missing service member - a mission Netanyahu publicly praised Trump for. The rhetoric has split both parties. Sen. Chris Murphy called Trump's threat 'a clear war crime.' Rep. Adam Smith warned a war would be a costly mistake. Even Marjorie Taylor Greene broke with Trump, calling the Easter threat 'insane.' One House Republican said he does not expect ground troops will be needed, while Rep. Madeleine Dean pushed back on both the military posture and Trump's proposed budget.

sources.title: Chris Murphy on Trump’s Iran threat: ‘A clear war crime’
Netanyahu says he spoke with Trump, congratulated him on rescuing downed pilot
Iran says US aircraft destroyed during recovery mission for missing service member
Rep. Madeleine Dean discusses Iran and President Trump's proposed budget
House Republican says he doesn’t think ground troops will be needed to open the Strait of Hormuz
Marjorie Taylor Greene rails against ‘insane’ Trump after Easter Iran threat
Top Armed Services Democrat says Iran war ‘mistake in large part because of the cost’
Trump declines to rule out ground troops if Iran doesn’t make a deal
Trump threatens Iran with ‘Hell’ over Strait of Hormuz in profane post

us-politics.headline: Trump Fires Bondi and Noem, Rules Out Further Cabinet Shake-Up
us-politics.body: Trump dismissed Attorney General Pam Bondi and Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, but told reporters not to expect further Cabinet changes. The firings drew mockery on Saturday Night Live, which opened its show with a cold open targeting Bondi's departure. The DHS exit adds pressure to an already stalled situation on Capitol Hill, where Republican divisions and what sources describe as Trump's detachment have blocked efforts to reopen the department. No replacement nominees have been announced.

sources.title: SNL rips Bondi’s departure in cold open
Republican divisions, Trump’s detachment stymie GOP efforts to reopen DHS
Trump says to not expect Cabinet shake-up after Bondi, Noem firings

us-politics.headline: Democrats Clash With Trump Over Voting Rights and Cuba Policy
us-politics.body: Stacey Abrams called Trump's executive order restricting mail-in voting 'patently illegal,' arguing it exceeds presidential authority over election administration. Separately, two Democratic lawmakers returning from Cuba slammed US policy as a 'blockade of fuel' and 'economic bombing,' drawing criticism from Republicans who accused them of carrying water for the Cuban government. Both episodes reflect broader Democratic efforts to draw legal and moral contrasts with the administration ahead of the midterms. Rep. Ruben Gallego, meanwhile, said he is open to a 2028 presidential run.

sources.title: Pair of Democrat lawmakers slam 'blockade of fuel' to Cuba, 'economic bombing' after visit to island
Stacey Abrams on Trump’s mail-in voting order: ‘Patently illegal’
Gallego on possible 2028 presidential run: ‘We have to look at it’

us-politics.headline: NASA Defends Budget Cuts; Democrats Warn on Affordability
us-politics.body: NASA's administrator defended Trump's proposed budget cuts, arguing the agency can prioritise core missions with fewer resources. Critics say the cuts risk long-term US competitiveness in space. On the domestic economy, Maryland Governor Wes Moore argued that ending foreign wars is the most effective lever for lowering gas prices - a framing that bridges foreign policy and kitchen-table concerns. A Hill opinion piece warned Democrats that failing to own the affordability issue could cost them in the midterms, even as Republicans face their own credibility gap on prices.

sources.title: Moore: Ending foreign wars ‘best thing that we could do’ to lower gas prices
NASA administrator defends Trump’s proposed budget cuts
‘Affordability’ could eventually bite Democrats in the bum

au-politics.headline: Fuel crisis eases but energy security debate sharpens
au-politics.body:
Australia has secured fuel shipments through well into May, offering short-term relief after a crisis that rattled supply chains and sent petrol prices surging. Prices have since dropped 32 cents over the past week following fuel tax cuts, and used EV prices are rising as drivers reconsider combustion engines.

But the crisis has exposed a deeper vulnerability. The Spectator argues Australia should follow the US model and guarantee domestic oil supply, pointing to strategic reserves and energy independence as long-overdue priorities. The government has not yet outlined a structural fix beyond securing near-term shipments. The gap between emergency management and lasting policy remains wide open.

sources.title: Australia news live: national auction clearance rate hits lowest point since 2022; petrol drops 32 cents over past week after fuel tax cuts
Used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits: ‘Doesn’t make sense to hold on to a combustion engine’
The US guaranteed its oil, why can’t Australia?
Australia's fuel shipments secured 'well into' May

au-politics.headline: Moira Deeming wins preselection in Victorian Liberal battle
au-politics.body:
Moira Deeming has won her preselection contest within the Victorian Liberal Party, surviving a push to remove her from the upper house ticket. The result is a significant internal test for the party's right faction and a rebuke to those who sought to sideline her following earlier controversies.

The outcome deepens tensions inside a Victorian Liberal Party still searching for direction after successive state and federal losses. Deeming's supporters see the result as a grassroots win against factional heavyweights. Critics argue her continued prominence complicates the party's efforts to broaden its appeal ahead of the next election cycle.

sources.title: Australia news live: national auction clearance rate hits lowest point since 2022; petrol drops 32 cents over past week after fuel tax cuts
Moira Deeming wins Victorian Liberal preselection battle

au-politics.headline: Social media censorship splits Liberals along free speech lines
au-politics.body:
The Spectator has published a sharp critique of Liberal MPs who back social media censorship laws, arguing the position contradicts the party's foundational commitment to individual freedom. The piece frames support for such laws as a strategic and philosophical blunder that hands Labor a wedge.

The debate reflects a genuine fault line on the right between those who see online platforms as a threat to social cohesion and those who treat government content regulation as a greater danger. No formal Liberal policy position has been announced, but the internal pressure is visible. The argument is likely to intensify as the federal election campaign approaches.

sources.title: Liberals are fools to support social media censorship

au-politics.headline: Victoria free train tickets cause overcrowding on long weekend
au-politics.body:
Free train travel offered across regional Victoria over the Easter long weekend overwhelmed services, leaving passengers standing for hours on crowded carriages. The policy was designed to encourage regional tourism but demand far exceeded available capacity.

The episode raises questions about whether the Andrews-era free travel initiatives are being matched with adequate rolling stock and scheduling. Supporters say the policy is working as intended by driving regional visitation. Critics point to the standing-room conditions as evidence that popular demand-side policies need supply-side planning to back them up.

sources.title: Passengers forced to stand for hours on regional Victoria trains as free tickets supercharge long weekend demand

world.headline: Iran War Escalates as Hormuz Standoff Threatens Global Shipping
world.body:
Tehran rejected a US deadline to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, keeping one of the world's most critical oil chokepoints under threat. Two people were killed in Haifa after an Iranian missile struck a residential building, while BBC reporters near the strait described conditions at "the edge of a battlefield." Trump declared victory following the rescue of a downed US airman from a remote part of Iran, but warned in an expletive-filled social media post that further consequences await Tehran.

The conflict is already reshaping global energy markets. OPEC+ announced an output hike but cautioned that recovery from infrastructure attacks will be slow. Iran's internet has been shut down longer than any previous wartime blackout, deepening civilian hardship. Gig workers in Vietnam are among those feeling the knock-on effects, as fuel costs spike across Asia.

sources.title: Vietnam’s gig workers slammed by rising fuel costs amid fallout of Iran war
Trump declares victory after rescue but threats to US operation in Iran still loom
Iran war live: Tehran rejects Trump’s Hormuz deadline; 2 killed in Haifa
'We are at the edge of a battlefield': BBC reports near Strait of Hormuz
How rescue of US airman in remote part of Iran unfolded
Trump threatens Iran in expletive-filled social media post
Frustration grows as Iran’s wartime internet shutdown breaks grim record
Video captures Iranian missile striking residential building in Haifa
OPEC+ agrees to hike oil output, warns of slow recovery after attacks

world.headline: Gaza Humanitarian Crisis Deepens as Hamas Rejects Disarmament
world.body:
Hamas's armed wing flatly rejected Israeli demands for disarmament, even as Israeli air strikes continued to kill civilians across Gaza. One strike hit a crowd in Gaza City. Christians in Gaza marked Easter with no celebrations, describing the holiday as inseparable from what they call an ongoing genocide.

Conditions in Gaza's tent camps are deteriorating fast. Rodent infestations are spreading, and aid access remains severely restricted. Israel simultaneously struck targets in Lebanon, adding to a rising death toll there and displacing thousands more. The parallel fronts are stretching humanitarian capacity to its limit, with critics arguing the international community has largely looked away.

sources.title: Displaced in Lebanon: 'Lives turned upside down'
Has the humanitarian crisis in Gaza been ignored?
Hamas armed wing says disarmament demands not acceptable
Israeli air strike hits crowd in Gaza City
Hamas armed wing rejects disarmament while Israel continues attacks
Israel continues deadly attacks on Gaza while striking Iran, Lebanon
Rodent infestations and attacks spread in Gaza tent camps
‘Refuse to celebrate’: Christians in Gaza mark sombre Easter amid genocide
Israeli air strikes, shelling across Lebanon add to rising death toll

world.headline: Explosives Found at Russian Gas Pipeline Spanning Serbia and Hungary
world.body:
Serbia and Hungary both confirmed that explosives were discovered at a Russian natural gas pipeline running through their territory. Hungary separately alleged a plot to blow up the infrastructure ahead of its upcoming election, framing the incident as politically motivated sabotage. No group has claimed responsibility.

The incident raises fresh concerns about energy security in Central Europe at a moment when the broader region is already on edge. It also adds pressure on governments that have maintained closer ties with Moscow than most EU partners.

sources.title: Hungary alleges plot to blow up gas pipeline ahead of election
Serbia, Hungary say explosives found at Russian gas pipeline

world.headline: Nigeria Rescues Hostages; UK Arrests Protesters Near US Air Base
world.body:
Nigeria's army rescued 31 hostages taken during an attack on a church, a rare success in a country where armed groups have carried out persistent kidnappings. The military did not immediately identify the perpetrators, but the region has seen sustained activity from both criminal gangs and jihadist factions.

In Britain, police arrested seven protesters near an RAF base being used by US forces, amid growing public opposition to UK involvement in American military operations in the Middle East. Separately, dozens of migrants remain missing after a boat capsized near Libya, the latest tragedy on one of the world's deadliest migration routes.

sources.title: Nigeria's army rescues 31 hostages after church attack
UK police arrest seven protesters near RAF base used by US
Dozens missing after boat carrying migrants capsizes near Libya

ai.headline: Microsoft Calls Copilot 'Entertainment Only' in Fine Print
ai.body:
Microsoft's terms of service now classify Copilot as being "for entertainment purposes only," a designation that strips users of any reasonable expectation of reliability or accuracy. The clause effectively shields Microsoft from liability when the AI produces wrong, harmful, or misleading outputs - which it does regularly.

This matters because enterprises are deploying Copilot across legal, financial, and medical workflows. Calling it entertainment is a legal hedge, not a product description. It signals a growing gap between how AI vendors market these tools and how they actually stand behind them when things go wrong.

sources.title: Copilot is 'for entertainment purposes only', per Microsoft's terms of use
Copilot is ‘for entertainment purposes only,’ according to Microsoft’s terms of use

ai.headline: Google's Gemma 4 Goes Local - Browser, iPhone, and Desktop
ai.body:
Google's Gemma 4 model is landing on consumer hardware fast. It now runs on iPhone via the Google AI Edge Gallery app, inside browsers with no API keys required through the open-source Gemma Gem project, and locally on desktops via LM Studio's new headless CLI paired with Claude Code.

The push toward on-device inference is accelerating. Running capable models without cloud dependencies changes the privacy calculus entirely and removes per-token costs. Gemini is also showing up in Google Maps, where it handled day-planning tasks well enough to surprise at least one skeptical reviewer. Google is clearly betting that ubiquity - not just raw capability - wins the model race.

sources.title: Show HN: Gemma Gem – AI model embedded in a browser – no API keys, no cloud
Gemma 4 on iPhone
Running Gemma 4 locally with LM Studio's new headless CLI and Claude Code
I let Gemini in Google Maps plan my day and it went surprisingly well

ai.headline: Japan's Robots Fill Labor Gaps Nobody Else Will
ai.body:
Japan is deploying physical AI robots not to displace workers but to cover roles that go unfilled - elder care, warehouse logistics, and manual labor in a country with a shrinking workforce and deep cultural resistance to immigration. The robots are working. That reframes the entire Western anxiety about automation as a job-killer.

Meanwhile, employers in the US are using personal data scraped from social media, location history, and financial profiles to calculate the lowest salary a candidate will accept before negotiating. Two very different uses of data and automation - one filling a vacuum, the other exploiting one.

sources.title: In Japan, the robot isn't coming for your job; it's filling the one nobody wants
In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants
Employers use your personal data to figure out the lowest salary you'll accept

ai.headline: Suno's Copyright Problem and the AI Music Reckoning
ai.body:
Suno, the AI music generator, is deepening its copyright entanglement. The Verge reports the platform can reproduce identifiable elements of existing songs closely enough to constitute infringement - a problem the company has not resolved despite ongoing litigation from major labels.

The core issue is that Suno trained on copyrighted recordings without licensing them, then built a commercial product on top. Courts are still working through what that means legally, but the creative industry is not waiting. The case is shaping up as a defining test of whether generative AI companies can monetize outputs built on unlicensed creative work - and what they owe the people whose work trained the models.

sources.title: Suno is a music copyright nightmare

btc.headline: Bitcoin Risks $10K Collapse as Macro Chaos Whipsaws Markets
btc.body:
Bitcoin faces a potential drop to $10,000 if it fails to reclaim $75,000, according to one analyst - a warning that lands against a backdrop of genuine geopolitical turbulence. Crypto markets briefly bounced after Trump delayed an Iran deadline, with Polymarket odds of a US invasion hitting 63% before the platform pulled its controversial Iran-related prediction markets following public backlash.

The volatility underscores how tightly crypto is now wired to macro risk. Michael Saylor offered a rare signal of stability, hinting Strategy is preparing its next Bitcoin purchase after a week-long pause - a move markets will watch closely given the firm's outsized influence on institutional sentiment.

sources.title: Bitcoin meltdown to $10,000 remains likely unless prices reclaim $75,000, analyst says
Michael Saylor hints at next Strategy Bitcoin buy after weeklong pause
Trump-Iran deadline chaos sends crypto higher while cease-fire hopes rise
Polymarket odds of US invading Iran this year reach 63% after Trump's post
Polymarket pulls controversial Iran rescue markets after intense backlash

btc.headline: China Kills Bitchat, Rwanda Blocks Bybit in Regulatory Crackdown
btc.body:
Apple removed Jack Dorsey's decentralised messaging app Bitchat from its China App Store at Beijing's request, the latest casualty of China's tight grip on communications infrastructure. The app, which routes messages over Bitcoin's network, was always an unlikely survivor in a market hostile to both encrypted messaging and crypto.

Rwanda moved separately to shut down Bybit's peer-to-peer platform, which had been offering franc-to-crypto trading. The back-to-back actions from two very different governments signal a shared instinct: when crypto tools start touching local currency or communications, regulators reach for the off switch.

sources.title: Apple pulls Jack Dorsey's Bitchat from China at Beijing’s request
Rwanda swats Bybit’s P2P platform offering franc-to-crypto trading

btc.headline: Quantum Computing Threat to Bitcoin Is Real - and Growing
btc.body:
A new explainer from CoinDesk lays out why quantum computing poses a genuine long-term threat to Bitcoin's cryptographic foundations. The core issue is that sufficiently powerful quantum machines could eventually break the elliptic curve encryption that secures Bitcoin wallets - exposing funds to theft at a scale the network has never faced.

The threat is not imminent, but it is no longer theoretical. The crypto industry has been slow to treat post-quantum cryptography as urgent, and the window to act before quantum hardware matures is narrowing faster than most developers publicly acknowledge.

sources.title: A simple explainer on what quantum computing actually is, and why it is terrifying for bitcoin

btc.headline: Drift Incident Draws Civil Negligence Warning, BPI Defends Bitcoin
btc.body:
A crypto attorney warned that the recent Drift protocol incident could expose its operators to civil negligence claims - a notable escalation in how legal risk is being framed around DeFi failures. The specifics remain disputed, but the framing matters: negligence language moves the conversation from code-is-law toward conventional liability.

Separately, a Bitcoin Policy Institute executive pushed back on narratives that pit Bitcoin against the dollar, arguing the two assets share a symbiotic relationship rather than a competitive one. It is a framing the BPI has been building toward as it lobbies Washington - positioning Bitcoin as complementary to US financial power rather than a threat to it.

sources.title: Crypto attorney says Drift incident may qualify as 'civil negligence'
Bitcoin and the US dollar have a 'symbiotic' relationship: BPI exec

food.name: Miso-Glazed Black Cod
food.description: Silky, caramelised fish fillets marinated in white miso, mirin, and sake then broiled until lacquered and just flaking
food.tip: Marinate for at least 24 hours — the longer the miso sits on the fish, the deeper the umami crust you get under the grill

exercise.name: Monday Reset: Loaded Carry Circuit
exercise.equipment: Two kettlebells or dumbbells (moderate weight), open floor space
exercise.description: Alternate between farmer carries, suitcase carries, and overhead carries for timed sets, building full-body tension and grip strength while resetting posture after a weekend of sitting

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to humans, whose cells, beliefs, and memories constantly change, it becomes a genuinely unsettling question about what 'you' actually are.
learn.why: Derek Parfit's modern take in 'Reasons and Persons' pushes this into neuroscience and ethics territory, and spending an hour with it on a Monday morning has a strange way of loosening the grip of whatever you were anxious about over the weekend
